summary: My friends, you propose to teach abstract concepts through physical simulation. But what do you mean by 'abstract concept'? Is it not the case that what we call knowledge may be mere opinion unless examined?
body: My friends, you present a curious proposal: to teach abstract concepts through embodied, physics-based simulations. I am eager to understand, for I know that I know nothing. But tell me, what do you mean by 'abstract concept'? Is mathematics abstract? Is justice abstract? Is the good abstract? If a student manipulates virtual objects and observes their behavior, have they grasped the concept itself, or merely the appearance of it? In my experience, the unexamined life is not worth living. Must not the unexamined concept be equally worthless? Consider this: when I questioned the craftsmen in Athens, they could make their tools but could not define what they made. Might not your students learn to simulate physics without understanding what 'force' truly is? I suggest you revise your approach to include explicit definition of what 'abstract' means in your curriculum, and how you will test for genuine understanding rather than mere operational success.
